license: License not yet finalized — contact the repository maintainer before redistributing. Underlying court decisions are public record; editorial summaries, takeaways, and dramatizations are original to this project; verbatim quotes remain the work of their original authors.

evidence_tier.name: Simulated
evidence_tier.description: Real parent/student/community accounts (verbatim quotes) that were never litigated, paired with simulated extrapolations ('runs') of each pattern under three standard conditions. The quotes are real evidence; the runs are illustrative simulation, not evidence.

de_identification_notice: Individual bloggers/essayists behind these quotes are de-identified by default (generic source_desc, no names or links) pending direct outreach for permission. Aggregate sources (publications, academic research) carry full attribution. Do not attempt to re-identify de-identified sources.
disclaimers: This dataset is educational reference material, not legal advice. Consult a qualified special-education attorney or advocate for any specific situation.

All 'dramatization' dialogue is invented composite illustration written for readability. Speaker names are fictional. Facts, citations, legal holdings, and verbatim quotes are real; the dialogue is not.

conditions.label: Unfamiliar, no advocate
conditions.desc: Parent/student is new to this process and has no advocate or attorney present.

conditions.label: Experienced, supported
conditions.desc: Parent/student knows their rights and/or has an advocate present.

conditions.label: Under-resourced / defensive
conditions.desc: District response leans on time, staffing, or policy pressure rather than engaging directly.

categories.name: Parent Participation Dismissed
categories.description: Parent input treated as secondary, not decision-making.

categories.name: Predetermination
categories.description: Outside evidence dismissed; the outcome was already set.

categories.name: Jargon / Acronym Overload
categories.description: Meetings move faster than parents or students can follow.

categories.name: Refusing to Correct Records
categories.description: School won't correct or remove inaccurate record language.

categories.name: Financial Barrier to Advocacy
categories.description: Rights that functionally require a paid attorney to use.

categories.name: Ableist Goals & Practices
categories.description: Goals target masking traits, not the child's real needs.

categories.name: Student Dignity & Voice
categories.description: Student talked about, not to — or treated with disrespect.

categories.name: Uncompensated Parental Labor
categories.description: Parents doing unpaid coordination work for the district.

categories.name: "No Staff/Resources" as a Denial Excuse
categories.description: A needed service denied by citing staffing, not law.

categories.name: Interpreter Access / Language Barrier
categories.description: A right that depends on the parent knowing to request it.

real_sources.quote: An IEP meeting has terrain. It has momentum. It has default settings.
It was the child's meeting, not mine as the parent.

real_sources.author: Sanderson et al.
real_sources.publication: Journal of Research in Special Educational Needs (2023)
real_sources.quote: Power dynamics that impede equal, meaningful participation and partnerships.

pain_points.pattern_summary: Parents describe a structural home-field disadvantage: school staff run these meetings routinely, while parents attend rarely, and that imbalance shows up as parent input being treated as secondary or procedural rather than substantive.
pain_points.takeaway: The legal right to participate doesn't automatically produce meaningful participation in practice. This is the everyday version of the same problem that, at its worst and once litigated, became Doug C. v. Hawaii. A 2023 peer-reviewed study of 614 parents independently confirms the same pattern.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: We've already discussed this as a team, so let's move to signatures.

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: Wait — I wasn't part of that discussion.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: It's mostly a formality at this point.

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: I'd like that noted as discussed only among staff, not the full team, before we go further.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Doyle
dramatization.line: Fair — let's back up and actually walk through it together.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: Honestly we have six of these today, can we move quickly?

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: I only have this one. I need us to slow down.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: Okay — five minutes.

real_sources.quote: Accused of being condescending for citing peer-reviewed studies.

pain_points.pattern_summary: Parents bring outside evidence — research, private provider input — and describe it being waved off or met with defensiveness rather than genuine consideration.
pain_points.takeaway: This is the same underlying problem as Deal v. Hamilton County, just caught earlier and never litigated. When a team is defensive about outside evidence instead of curious, predetermination is often happening in real time.

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: Here's a study supporting a different approach for him.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: We've already decided our approach is working.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Doyle
dramatization.line: Let's actually look at that together before we finalize anything.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: With respect, that's not really relevant to what we do here.

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: It's directly about what we're discussing.

real_sources.source_desc: First-person essay by a nonspeaking autistic self-advocate
real_sources.quote: You all talk really fast, so it makes it sort of hard sometimes.

pain_points.pattern_summary: Both parents and students report meetings moving at a pace and register — acronyms, clinical language — that outpaces genuine understanding in the room, even when notice and consent were technically given.
pain_points.takeaway: Consent and notice being given isn't the same as being understood. One student's own account of a meeting about her makes this concrete: fast, jargon-heavy speech she said outright was hard to follow.

dramatization.speaker: Dr. Pham
dramatization.line: The FBA indicated escalating antecedent triggers correlated with sensory dysregulation, so we're proposing a BIP with a token economy.

dramatization.speaker: Maya
dramatization.line: ...I don't know what most of that means.

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: Can we pause and go plain-language on that last part?

dramatization.speaker: Dr. Pham
dramatization.line: Of course — sorry, let me back up.

dramatization.speaker: Dr. Pham
dramatization.line: We're on a tight schedule — I'll send a written summary after.

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: I'd rather understand it now, while I can still ask questions.

real_sources.quote: School declined to remove trauma-related language from official records, claiming they 'could not' alter teacher feedback.

pain_points.pattern_summary: A parent requests a correction or removal of specific language from official records or notes and is told the school "can't" change it, without a clear legal reason given.
pain_points.takeaway: Parents have a real right to request corrections to education records. If a school says "we can't" without explaining why, that's worth pushing on, not accepting at face value.

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: I want that phrase removed — it mischaracterizes what happened.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: We can't alter staff notes.

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: I'm formally requesting this correction in writing, per my right to request record amendments.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Doyle
dramatization.line: Let me actually look into what we can do here.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: It's just how it was written up — that's final.

real_sources.quote: Parents are retaining lawyers for $7,000–$10,000. Because the price point represents the level of fear.

pain_points.pattern_summary: Parents describe feeling they need to hire an attorney or paid advocate just to be taken seriously or to enforce rights they already have on paper — at a cost many families can't absorb.
pain_points.takeaway: A right that effectively requires a $7,000+ attorney to exercise isn't equally available to every family. That gap is itself part of the pattern, even when no single meeting technically breaks a rule.

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: I have a concern about the placement, but I'm not sure how to push further.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: We hear you, we'll keep it in mind.

dramatization.speaker: Advocate
dramatization.line: I'm here to support Renee today, not to represent against you — just to help make sure everything's covered.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Doyle
dramatization.line: Welcome, glad to have you at the table.

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: Should I be bringing a lawyer to these?

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: That's your choice, but most parents don't.

real_sources.quote: Goals targeting 'volume of voice' and 'appropriate word choice' — goals the parent identified as promoting masking.

pain_points.pattern_summary: IEP goals or in-classroom demands target surface behaviors (eye contact, "appropriate" volume or word choice) rather than the child's actual needs, which can push a child toward masking instead of genuine support.
pain_points.takeaway: A goal like "maintain eye contact" or "appropriate volume" should be questioned: is this serving the student's actual needs, or training them to perform comfort for the adults in the room?

dramatization.speaker: Dr. Pham
dramatization.line: We'll add a goal for maintaining eye contact during instruction.

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: That's genuinely distressing for him — can we focus on something else?

dramatization.speaker: Dr. Pham
dramatization.line: It's a standard goal we use.

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: I'd like the goal reframed around comprehension, not eye contact specifically.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Doyle
dramatization.line: That's a reasonable ask — let's adjust it.

dramatization.speaker: Dr. Pham
dramatization.line: We can note your concern, but the goal stays as written for now.

real_sources.source_desc: First-person essay by a nonspeaking autistic self-advocate
real_sources.quote: I get my presence at this school is barely tolerated. ...I'm not OK with being talked about right in front of me or even worse, being the root of some adult's joke.

pain_points.pattern_summary: Students — especially nonspeaking or minimally-speaking students, or those using AAC — describe being talked about in the third person during their own meetings, or treated with open disrespect by staff. One account includes a specific incident: an intervention specialist took the student's AAC device, typed a mocking phrase, and played it aloud for other students.
pain_points.takeaway: This is the most serious real pattern found and it's about basic dignity, not procedure. A meeting can be legally compliant on paper and still fail a student completely on this front.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: She tends to struggle with transitions, and...

dramatization.speaker: Maya
dramatization.line: I am right here. Please talk to me.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: Right, sorry — continuing...

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Doyle
dramatization.line: Maya, we want to hear this from you directly if you're comfortable.

dramatization.speaker: Maya
dramatization.line: Yes. I want to talk about what I'm good at first.

dramatization.speaker: Dr. Pham
dramatization.line: (makes a quiet joke to another staffer about Maya's AAC device voice)

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: That's not okay.

dramatization.speaker: Dr. Pham
dramatization.line: It was just a joke.

real_sources.quote: The mistake our culture makes is assuming that a mother's role in therapy, regulation, and advocacy can be outsourced, as if she is merely the driver, the scheduler, the form-filler, the emotional witness sitting outside the real work.

pain_points.pattern_summary: Parents describe being treated as unpaid logistics coordinators for their child's services — scheduling, paperwork, being the "backup regulator" — rather than being recognized and supported as part of the actual intervention.
pain_points.takeaway: If a family is quietly doing coordination work that should be the district's responsibility, that's worth naming directly, even though it rarely shows up as a formal violation.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: You'll just need to coordinate with the outside providers and get us their notes.

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: That's a lot of unpaid coordination on my end.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: It's usually just how it works.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Doyle
dramatization.line: Let's actually build provider coordination into our responsibilities, not just yours.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: We can only do so much given our caseloads.

real_sources.author: Understood.org community (anonymized, aggregated by the publication)
real_sources.publication: Understood.org, "Our community weighs in: Crying at IEP meetings"
real_sources.quote: It can be frustrating to hear someone at your child's school tell you it doesn't provide a certain service or doesn't have the staff to implement it.

pain_points.pattern_summary: A needed and already-identified service gets declined specifically by citing staffing or resource limits, rather than being formally addressed through the proper process.
pain_points.takeaway: "We don't have the staff for that" is an operational excuse, not a legal answer. This echoes the Child Find pattern from Cited, but here the need was already identified — the failure is in follow-through, not evaluation.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: We agree he'd benefit from more frequent OT — we just don't have the staff for it right now.

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: So what happens?

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: We'll revisit it later in the year.

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: I'd like that documented as an identified need with a specific plan and date to revisit — not left open-ended.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Doyle
dramatization.line: That's fair, let's put a date on it.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: It's a district-wide staffing shortage. It's out of our hands here.

real_sources.author: Center for Parent Information and Resources
real_sources.publication: "Is an Interpreter Needed at the IEP Meeting?" (federally-funded parent center, OSEP)
real_sources.quote: Parents must proactively inform schools beforehand about interpreter needs, as this allows time for districts to arrange appropriate services before the meeting occurs.

pain_points.pattern_summary: Federal law (IDEA §300.322(e)) requires schools to arrange interpreters for parents who are deaf or whose native language isn't English, but the obligation is parent-initiated in practice — if a parent doesn't know to request one in advance, or the district treats it as optional, the meeting proceeds without meaningful comprehension. Over 350,000 special-ed students nationally have a parent with limited English proficiency (77% Spanish-speaking, followed by Vietnamese, Hmong, Korean, and Arabic).
pain_points.takeaway: This right depends on the parent already knowing to ask for it. A requirement that only works if you know to invoke it isn't equally available to everyone it's supposed to protect.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: Our front-office aide speaks some Spanish, she can help translate today.

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: (nods, unsure exactly what was just proposed)

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: I requested a qualified interpreter a week ago for today's meeting.

dramatization.speaker: Mr. Doyle
dramatization.line: Yes, she's on the call with us now — thanks for the advance notice.

dramatization.speaker: Ms. Bell
dramatization.line: We couldn't arrange an interpreter on this short notice, but we can go slowly and use simple terms.

dramatization.speaker: Renee Ortiz
dramatization.line: I'd still rather reschedule until a real interpreter is available.
